title: SUFE (Slipped Upper Femoral Epiphysis)
oneLiner: A Salter-Harris type I fracture through the proximal femoral physis in adolescents (10–16 yrs), usually obese or endocrine-abnormal; delays cause avascular necrosis (AVN).

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Septic arthritis of the hip** — can mimic SUFE and progresses to joint destruction/sepsis within hours  
  - Single finding: **refusal to bear weight + fever >38.5 °C + toxic appearance** (vs. SUFE where temperature is normal or low-grade)
- **Avascular necrosis (AVN)** — not immediate, but **unstable SUFE** can critically impair femoral head blood flow within 12–24 hours if not protected (non–weight‑bear, urgent fixation)  
  - Single finding: **unable to straight‑leg raise + severe pain on any passive movement** (especially internal rotation) – suspect unstable slip → urgent orthopaedic senior review

---
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms** (3–5 you’ll actually see):
  - Hip/groin pain (may be referred to **anterior thigh or knee** – classic pitfall)
  - Limp (antalgic or Trendelenburg)
  - Recent or acute inability to bear weight (unstable slip)
  - Onset: weeks to months (chronic) or sudden (acute/chronic‑on‑acute)
- **Key exam findings**:
  - Hip held in **external rotation** at rest
  - **Limited internal rotation** (most reliable sign; often <10°)
  - Pain at extremes of flexion/rotation
  - Leg length discrepancy (if chronic; affected leg shorter and externally rotated)
  - Check **both hips** – bilateral SUFE in ~20–40%

---
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
| Likelihood | Condition | 1‑line differentiator |
|------------|-----------|----------------------|
| Most common | Transient synovitis (irritable hip) | Afebrile, no systemic upset, painful but often can partially weight‑bear; resolves in 3–7 days |
| Second | Septic arthritis | **Fever**, CRP/ESR markedly raised, refusal to bear weight at all, joint effusion on ultrasound |
| Third | Perthes disease (LCPD) | Age 4–8 (not adolescent); radiograph shows femoral head flattening/ fragmentation |
| Fourth | Femoral neck fracture | High‑energy trauma or pathological; distinct fracture line on X‑ray |
| Less common | Avascular necrosis (sickle cell, steroid) | Bilateral, older child/adult; MRI shows no slip but marrow oedema |
| **Splitter** | **Ability to bear weight (even with limp) + normal temperature** → SUFE/transient synovitis vs. **complete non‑weight‑bearing + fever** → septic arthritis | |

---
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - **Ensure patient is non‑weight‑bearing** – immediately protect the physis
  - Assess temperature, pulse, NEWS2
  - **Do NOT force internal rotation** – can displace an unstable slip
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, CRP, ESR (to rule out septic arthritis – if elevated, proceed to hip aspiration)
  - Consider blood cultures if febrile
- **Imaging:**
  - **First‑line:** AP pelvis + **frog‑leg lateral** of both hips (essential – shows the slip on lateral view)
  - **Second‑line:** Ultrasound – if septic arthritis suspected (effusion + capsule distension); **MRI** – if X‑rays equivocal and high clinical suspicion
- **Special tests:**
  - **Hip aspiration** if septic arthritis suspected – must be done under ultrasound/fluoroscopy
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **Loder classification** (stable vs unstable):
    - **Stable:** can bear weight with crutches → lower AVN risk (5–10%)
    - **Unstable:** cannot bear weight at all, even with assistance → AVN risk up to 50%
  - **Southwick slip angle** (severity): <30° mild, 30–50° moderate, >50° severe
- **Never‑miss test:** **Bilateral frog‑leg lateral pelvis** – missing the contralateral slip is a common serious error (20–40% bilateral). Also **ultrasound-guided hip aspiration** if septic arthritis is on the differential – do not start antibiotics before cultures.

---
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Non‑weight‑bearing** strictly (crutches, wheelchair)
  - Analgesia: paracetamol ± ibuprofen (see doses below)
  - **Urgent orthopaedic referral** – all SUFE require surgical fixation
  - If septic arthritis suspected: **do not delay orthopaedic review** for aspiration/arthrotomy
- **Ward (first 24–72 h):**
  - **Pre‑operative optimisation:** nil by mouth (NBM) for theatre
  - **Endocrine workup** if atypical (e.g. age <10, bilateral, very obese): TFTs, IGF‑1, cortisol, FSH/LH, bone age (assess for hypothyroidism, growth hormone deficiency, hypogonadism)
  - **Surgery:** percutaneous in‑situ screw fixation (one or two screws)
  - **Prophylactic antibiotics:** single dose cefazolin or flucloxacillin at induction (as per local antibiotic policy)
- **Long‑term / outpatient:**
  - Follow‑up at 6 weeks, 3 months, 6 months, and 1 year for clinical and radiographic surveillance (AVN, chondrolysis, worsening slip)
  - **Contralateral hip** – consider prophylactic pinning if skeletal immaturity remains (especially girls <12, boys <14)
  - Weight management / endocrine follow‑up if needed
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: **nil by mouth** for theatre – IV maintenance (Hartmann’s / Plasmalyte) if fasting >6 h
  - VTE prophylaxis: **not routinely needed** in paediatric SUFE (low risk; mobilising post‑op)
  - Diet: NBM from 6 hours pre‑op; otherwise normal
  - Observations: 4‑hourly routine (increase to 1‑hourly surgical obs post‑op for 4 h)

---
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First‑line analgesia:**
  - Paracetamol 15 mg/kg (max 1 g) PO/IV 4–6‑hourly (max 4 g/day)
  - Ibuprofen 10 mg/kg (max 600 mg) PO 6–8‑hourly (max 30 mg/kg/day) – avoid if suspected septic arthritis (masks fever)
- **Alternative:** oral morphine (0.2–0.5 mg/kg) if severe pain
- **Contraindications / interactions:**
  - NSAIDs with renal impairment, bleeding risk, or allergy
  - Avoid opiates if GCS low/unstable slip with head injury
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** [check local policy] for NSAIDs in renal impairment; paracetamol safe in normal doses if hepatic impairment mild
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving Ibuprofen before septic arthritis is ruled out** – must first obtain cultures/aspiration. **Also never force internal rotation to give analgesia** – can displace an unstable slip.

---
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - **Avascular necrosis (AVN)** – highest risk in unstable slips (up to 50%); presents with increasing pain, collapse on X‑ray at 6–12 months
  - **Chondrolysis** – rapid loss of articular cartilage; presents with stiffness, loss of movement; X‑ray shows joint space narrowing
  - **Further slip** – if fixation fails or non‑compliance with non‑weight‑bearing
- **Chronic:**
  - **Hip osteoarthritis** (early onset, especially if chondrolysis develops)
  - **Femoroacetabular impingement** (from residual deformity)
  - **Leg length discrepancy** (often 1–2 cm)
  - **Contralateral slip** (develops later in 20–40%)

---
sections.Prognosis:
- **Stable slips:** excellent – >90% return to normal function with low AVN risk (5–10%)
- **Unstable slips:** guarded – AVN rate up to 50%, often leading to early hip replacement in young adulthood
- **Chronic untreated slips** progress to severe deformity and early arthritis; mortality negligible

---
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
1. **Always obtain a frog‑leg lateral view of both hips** – without it, mild slips are easily missed on AP alone.
2. **Examine the contralateral hip** – bilateral involvement is common; consider prophylactic pinning.
3. **Suspect endocrine causes** if patient is young (<10 yrs), very obese, or has bilateral disease – arrange TFT, IGF‑1, cortisol before discharge.

---
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
1. **Do NOT diagnose “growing pains” or knee strain** without examining the hip and getting a frog‑leg lateral – referred knee pain is the classic missed SUFE.
2. **Do NOT force internal rotation** of the hip (especially under anaesthesia or in clinic) – can convert a stable slip to an unstable one, precipitating AVN.
3. **Do NOT discharge the patient without orthopaedic review** – every SUFE needs fixation; discharging with “rest and analgesia” delays surgery and risks further slip.

---
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - **Sudden worsening of pain** (unable to bear weight at all) – may indicate slip progression or AVN
  - **Fever >38.5 °C** (suggesting septic arthritis) – even if previously diagnosed as SUFE
  - **New inability to move the hip** (especially internal rotation) – urgent orthopaedic review
- **Follow‑up:**
  - Orthopaedic clinic within 2 weeks (for pre‑op planning if not yet fixed)
  - If already fixed: routine follow‑up at 6 weeks, 3 months, 6 months, 1 year
  - Endocrine clinic if workup abnormal

---
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call orthopaedic senior (reg/consultant) immediately if:**
  - Suspected **unstable SUFE** (cannot bear weight, severe pain, leg held in external rotation)
  - **Fever + septic arthritis** concern (even if SUFE confirmed) – needs urgent aspiration
- **Call crash team / ICU if:**
  - **NEWS2 ≥5** or **lactate >2 mmol/L** (sepsis from septic hip or post‑op complication)
  - **GCS <13** or respiratory compromise (rare, but consider under‑resuscitated septic arthritis or opiate overdose)
- **Escalate to senior if:**
  - **Bilateral SUFE** – discuss prophylactic pinning on contralateral side
  - **Atypical presentation** (age <10, endocrine features) – needs pituitary workup and endocrine input

rawMarkdown:
# SUFE (Slipped Upper Femoral Epiphysis)

**One-liner:** A Salter-Harris type I fracture through the proximal femoral physis in adolescents (10–16 yrs), usually obese or endocrine-abnormal; delays cause avascular necrosis (AVN).

---

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?

- **Septic arthritis of the hip** — can mimic SUFE and progresses to joint destruction/sepsis within hours  
  - Single finding: **refusal to bear weight + fever >38.5 °C + toxic appearance** (vs. SUFE where temperature is normal or low-grade)
- **Avascular necrosis (AVN)** — not immediate, but **unstable SUFE** can critically impair femoral head blood flow within 12–24 hours if not protected (non–weight‑bear, urgent fixation)  
  - Single finding: **unable to straight‑leg raise + severe pain on any passive movement** (especially internal rotation) – suspect unstable slip → urgent orthopaedic senior review

---

### Presentation

- **Cardinal symptoms** (3–5 you’ll actually see):
  - Hip/groin pain (may be referred to **anterior thigh or knee** – classic pitfall)
  - Limp (antalgic or Trendelenburg)
  - Recent or acute inability to bear weight (unstable slip)
  - Onset: weeks to months (chronic) or sudden (acute/chronic‑on‑acute)
- **Key exam findings**:
  - Hip held in **external rotation** at rest
  - **Limited internal rotation** (most reliable sign; often <10°)
  - Pain at extremes of flexion/rotation
  - Leg length discrepancy (if chronic; affected leg shorter and externally rotated)
  - Check **both hips** – bilateral SUFE in ~20–40%

---

### Differential Diagnosis

| Likelihood | Condition | 1‑line differentiator |
|------------|-----------|----------------------|
| Most common | Transient synovitis (irritable hip) | Afebrile, no systemic upset, painful but often can partially weight‑bear; resolves in 3–7 days |
| Second | Septic arthritis | **Fever**, CRP/ESR markedly raised, refusal to bear weight at all, joint effusion on ultrasound |
| Third | Perthes disease (LCPD) | Age 4–8 (not adolescent); radiograph shows femoral head flattening/ fragmentation |
| Fourth | Femoral neck fracture | High‑energy trauma or pathological; distinct fracture line on X‑ray |
| Less common | Avascular necrosis (sickle cell, steroid) | Bilateral, older child/adult; MRI shows no slip but marrow oedema |
| **Splitter** | **Ability to bear weight (even with limp) + normal temperature** → SUFE/transient synovitis vs. **complete non‑weight‑bearing + fever** → septic arthritis | |

---

### Investigations

- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - **Ensure patient is non‑weight‑bearing** – immediately protect the physis
  - Assess temperature, pulse, NEWS2
  - **Do NOT force internal rotation** – can displace an unstable slip
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, CRP, ESR (to rule out septic arthritis – if elevated, proceed to hip aspiration)
  - Consider blood cultures if febrile
- **Imaging:**
  - **First‑line:** AP pelvis + **frog‑leg lateral** of both hips (essential – shows the slip on lateral view)
  - **Second‑line:** Ultrasound – if septic arthritis suspected (effusion + capsule distension); **MRI** – if X‑rays equivocal and high clinical suspicion
- **Special tests:**
  - **Hip aspiration** if septic arthritis suspected – must be done under ultrasound/fluoroscopy
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **Loder classification** (stable vs unstable):
    - **Stable:** can bear weight with crutches → lower AVN risk (5–10%)
    - **Unstable:** cannot bear weight at all, even with assistance → AVN risk up to 50%
  - **Southwick slip angle** (severity): <30° mild, 30–50° moderate, >50° severe
- **Never‑miss test:** **Bilateral frog‑leg lateral pelvis** – missing the contralateral slip is a common serious error (20–40% bilateral). Also **ultrasound-guided hip aspiration** if septic arthritis is on the differential – do not start antibiotics before cultures.

---

### Management

- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Non‑weight‑bearing** strictly (crutches, wheelchair)
  - Analgesia: paracetamol ± ibuprofen (see doses below)
  - **Urgent orthopaedic referral** – all SUFE require surgical fixation
  - If septic arthritis suspected: **do not delay orthopaedic review** for aspiration/arthrotomy
- **Ward (first 24–72 h):**
  - **Pre‑operative optimisation:** nil by mouth (NBM) for theatre
  - **Endocrine workup** if atypical (e.g. age <10, bilateral, very obese): TFTs, IGF‑1, cortisol, FSH/LH, bone age (assess for hypothyroidism, growth hormone deficiency, hypogonadism)
  - **Surgery:** percutaneous in‑situ screw fixation (one or two screws)
  - **Prophylactic antibiotics:** single dose cefazolin or flucloxacillin at induction (as per local antibiotic policy)
- **Long‑term / outpatient:**
  - Follow‑up at 6 weeks, 3 months, 6 months, and 1 year for clinical and radiographic surveillance (AVN, chondrolysis, worsening slip)
  - **Contralateral hip** – consider prophylactic pinning if skeletal immaturity remains (especially girls <12, boys <14)
  - Weight management / endocrine follow‑up if needed
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: **nil by mouth** for theatre – IV maintenance (Hartmann’s / Plasmalyte) if fasting >6 h
  - VTE prophylaxis: **not routinely needed** in paediatric SUFE (low risk; mobilising post‑op)
  - Diet: NBM from 6 hours pre‑op; otherwise normal
  - Observations: 4‑hourly routine (increase to 1‑hourly surgical obs post‑op for 4 h)

---

### Drugs & Doses

- **First‑line analgesia:**
  - Paracetamol 15 mg/kg (max 1 g) PO/IV 4–6‑hourly (max 4 g/day)
  - Ibuprofen 10 mg/kg (max 600 mg) PO 6–8‑hourly (max 30 mg/kg/day) – avoid if suspected septic arthritis (masks fever)
- **Alternative:** oral morphine (0.2–0.5 mg/kg) if severe pain
- **Contraindications / interactions:**
  - NSAIDs with renal impairment, bleeding risk, or allergy
  - Avoid opiates if GCS low/unstable slip with head injury
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** [check local policy] for NSAIDs in renal impairment; paracetamol safe in normal doses if hepatic impairment mild
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving Ibuprofen before septic arthritis is ruled out** – must first obtain cultures/aspiration. **Also never force internal rotation to give analgesia** – can displace an unstable slip.

---

### Complications

- **Acute:**
  - **Avascular necrosis (AVN)** – highest risk in unstable slips (up to 50%); presents with increasing pain, collapse on X‑ray at 6–12 months
  - **Chondrolysis** – rapid loss of articular cartilage; presents with stiffness, loss of movement; X‑ray shows joint space narrowing
  - **Further slip** – if fixation fails or non‑compliance with non‑weight‑bearing
- **Chronic:**
  - **Hip osteoarthritis** (early onset, especially if chondrolysis develops)
  - **Femoroacetabular impingement** (from residual deformity)
  - **Leg length discrepancy** (often 1–2 cm)
  - **Contralateral slip** (develops later in 20–40%)

---

### Prognosis

- **Stable slips:** excellent – >90% return to normal function with low AVN risk (5–10%)
- **Unstable slips:** guarded – AVN rate up to 50%, often leading to early hip replacement in young adulthood
- **Chronic untreated slips** progress to severe deformity and early arthritis; mortality negligible

---

### Pearls (positive actions)

1. **Always obtain a frog‑leg lateral view of both hips** – without it, mild slips are easily missed on AP alone.
2. **Examine the contralateral hip** – bilateral involvement is common; consider prophylactic pinning.
3. **Suspect endocrine causes** if patient is young (<10 yrs), very obese, or has bilateral disease – arrange TFT, IGF‑1, cortisol before discharge.

---

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)

1. **Do NOT diagnose “growing pains” or knee strain** without examining the hip and getting a frog‑leg lateral – referred knee pain is the classic missed SUFE.
2. **Do NOT force internal rotation** of the hip (especially under anaesthesia or in clinic) – can convert a stable slip to an unstable one, precipitating AVN.
3. **Do NOT discharge the patient without orthopaedic review** – every SUFE needs fixation; discharging with “rest and analgesia” delays surgery and risks further slip.

---

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)

- **Return if:**
  - **Sudden worsening of pain** (unable to bear weight at all) – may indicate slip progression or AVN
  - **Fever >38.5 °C** (suggesting septic arthritis) – even if previously diagnosed as SUFE
  - **New inability to move the hip** (especially internal rotation) – urgent orthopaedic review
- **Follow‑up:**
  - Orthopaedic clinic within 2 weeks (for pre‑op planning if not yet fixed)
  - If already fixed: routine follow‑up at 6 weeks, 3 months, 6 months, 1 year
  - Endocrine clinic if workup abnormal

---

### Escalation Criteria

- **Call orthopaedic senior (reg/consultant) immediately if:**
  - Suspected **unstable SUFE** (cannot bear weight, severe pain, leg held in external rotation)
  - **Fever + septic arthritis** concern (even if SUFE confirmed) – needs urgent aspiration
- **Call crash team / ICU if:**
  - **NEWS2 ≥5** or **lactate >2 mmol/L** (sepsis from septic hip or post‑op complication)
  - **GCS <13** or respiratory compromise (rare, but consider under‑resuscitated septic arthritis or opiate overdose)
- **Escalate to senior if:**
  - **Bilateral SUFE** – discuss prophylactic pinning on contralateral side
  - **Atypical presentation** (age <10, endocrine features) – needs pituitary workup and endocrine input
